author: Polycarp of Smyrna
interpretation: Polycarp echoes the language of Hebrews in describing Christ as the one 'whom God raised from the dead and gave a seat at his right hand, to whom all things in heaven and on earth are subject.' While not a direct commentary, Polycarp's letter shows that the earliest post-apostolic communities read Christ's exaltation at God's right hand as the defining fact about his present status and universal authority.
context: Polycarp's appropriation of Hebrews' exaltation language demonstrates how quickly these formulations became standard in early Christian confession and worship.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom reads the opening as establishing a radical contrast between old and new revelation. God spoke 'in many times and many ways' through prophets — fragmentary, varied, mediated. Now God speaks through a Son — final, comprehensive, direct. The Son is not merely the latest in a series of messengers but the definitive self-expression of God, after whom no further revelation is needed or expected.
context: Chrysostom's Hebrews homilies were among his last works. His emphasis on the finality of revelation through the Son became important for later Protestant arguments against claims of continuing prophetic authority.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine uses Hebrews 1:3 — the Son as 'the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of his being' — as a key Trinitarian text. Just as radiance is inseparable from light and co-eternal with it, so the Son is inseparable from and co-eternal with the Father. The image is not produced after the original but exists simultaneously with it. There was never a time when the Father was without his radiance.
context: Augustine's light-radiance analogy, drawn from Hebrews 1:3, became one of the most enduring illustrations of the eternal generation of the Son in Western theology.

author: Anselm of Canterbury
interpretation: Anselm reads the superiority of the Son over the angels (Heb 1:4) as establishing why only the Son — not an angel or a prophet — could accomplish atonement. An angel is a creature and cannot offer satisfaction of infinite value. Only one who shares God's own nature can bridge the infinite distance between human sin and divine honor. The Son's exaltation after purification for sins demonstrates the completion of that satisfaction.
context: Anselm's satisfaction atonement theory draws directly on Hebrews' priestly Christology. The Son's unique dignity (above angels, above prophets) is the prerequisite for his unique mediatorial work.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin reads the contrast between prophetic and Son-mediated revelation as establishing the principle of progressive revelation culminating in finality. The prophets gave partial light; the Son is full daylight. Calvin insists that this does not diminish the Old Testament — the same God spoke through both — but it does establish that Christ is the interpretive key to all prior revelation. Everything in the Old Testament points forward to him.
context: Calvin's commentary on Hebrews was among his most careful exegetical works. His principle that Christ is the hermeneutical key to the Old Testament became a foundational Reformed hermeneutical principle.

author: Karl Barth
interpretation: Barth takes Hebrews 1:1–2 as the programmatic statement for all theology: God has spoken — past tense, completed action — in his Son. Revelation is not an ongoing process but a finished event centered in Christ, to which Scripture and preaching bear witness. Barth uses this passage to argue that theology's task is not to discover new truths about God but to attend faithfully to the truth already spoken in Christ.
context: Barth's use of Hebrews 1 as a foundation for his doctrine of revelation positioned the passage at the center of twentieth-century theological debate between neo-orthodoxy and liberalism.

author: Richard Bauckham
source_title: Jesus and the God of Israel
interpretation: Bauckham reads the exordium as placing Jesus within the unique divine identity. The Son is not merely God's messenger (like the prophets and angels) but shares God's own nature — he is the radiance of God's glory, he sustains all things by his powerful word, he sits at God's right hand. Each descriptor places the Son on the Creator side of the Creator-creature divide. Hebrews 1 is one of the New Testament's most explicit statements of high Christology.
context: Bauckham's divine-identity reading of Hebrews 1 reinforces his broader argument that the highest Christology is not a late development but is present in the earliest strands of Christian writing.
